title: Dog Bloody Vaginal Discharge Weeks After Heat: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog bleeding weeks after her heat cycle? Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how to identify signs of Pyometra.
content_html:
TL;DR: Vaginal discharge appearing 2–8 weeks after a dog's heat cycle is a hallmark sign of pyometra, a life-threatening uterine infection that requires immediate veterinary emergency care. Prompt treatment is essential to prevent systemic sepsis or a ruptured uterus.

What does vaginal discharge after a dog's heat cycle mean?

When a dog experiences bloody, brown, or foul-smelling vaginal discharge several weeks after her heat cycle concludes, it is a significant clinical sign. While the initial heat cycle involves bleeding, that should subside relatively quickly. Discharge appearing two to eight weeks later is often a symptom of Pyometra, a serious bacterial infection that causes the uterus to fill with pus. This occurs because the hormonal changes after heat make the uterine lining more susceptible to bacteria.

Is post-heat vaginal discharge a veterinary emergency?

  • This is considered a high-priority veterinary emergency as Pyometra is life-threatening and can lead to systemic sepsis or a ruptured uterus if left untreated.
  • Immediate medical intervention is required if your dog is also showing signs of increased thirst, frequent urination, lethargy, or loss of appetite.
  • Even if she appears otherwise normal, any discharge in the weeks following a heat cycle must be evaluated by a veterinarian immediately to rule out infection.

How can a photo of the discharge help the veterinary triage team?

  • Taking a clear photo of the discharge on the dog's fur or on the floor can be an invaluable tool for the triage team.
  • A photo allows the veterinarian to see the exact color, opacity, and consistency of the fluid—whether it is bright red blood, dark "tomato soup" colored fluid, or creamy yellow pus.
  • This visual evidence helps the staff determine the severity of the infection and can expedite her care the moment you walk through the clinic doors.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hemorrhagic vulvar discharge weeks after estrus in a dog warrants investigation into several differentials. Subinvolution of placental sites (SIPS) should be considered, especially in young bitches after their first litter; these animals are otherwise normal except for the hemorrhagic discharge. Other differentials include metritis, vaginitis, and cystitis, which must be ruled out. Ovarian remnant syndrome, indicative of functional ovarian tissue remaining after ovariohysterectomy, should also be considered. If pyometra is suspected, particularly with systemic illness signs, radiographs should be taken to assess for retained fetuses or placentas, and a hemogram may reveal leukocytosis. Vaginal hyperplasia, resulting in a mass protruding from the vulva due to estrogenic stimulation during proestrus and estrus, may also be a cause.

Chapter: Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1399)

faqs.question: Is it normal for a dog to bleed 4 weeks after heat?
faqs.answer: No, it is not normal. Bleeding or discharge that starts or persists weeks after a heat cycle has ended is a primary indicator of a uterine infection called Pyometra.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because Pyometra can progress to a life-threatening state very quickly, you should seek care at an emergency animal hospital immediately rather than waiting for an appointment.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of Pyometra?
faqs.answer: The most common signs include vaginal discharge, increased water consumption, frequent urination, lethargy, vomiting, and a bloated or painful abdomen.
